title: 5 Safety Mistakes Injectors Make.
excerpt: Five safety mistakes that you might be making that lead to Patient injury side effects complications disappointments and onear reviews and all of the other things that go with it and how to avoid them that's what we're covering on this show make sure you pay attention because thi…

segments.text: five safety mistakes that you might be making that lead to Patient injury side effects complications disappointments and onear reviews and all of the other things that go with it and how to avoid them that's what we're covering on this show make sure you pay attention because this is going to save you a lot of time and effort so what happens if you don't have a focus on safety it's not just about the complications it's not about the vasular inclusion that we all worry about what actually happens is your day-to-day noise rate of negative outcomes increases so it's more bruises it's more swelling it's more patients anxious about things it's more talking trying to get out of problems it's more reassurance it's a lot more leg work for your front of house staff if you're employing people having to reassure people that bruise will eventually go away before the event that's happening a focus on reducing all your complication rates and trying to make everything as safe as possible actually leads to an easier to run Clinic because there's just less emotion in your day-to-day work because most people just go away with the result that they were expecting and you

don't have to deal with negative consequences so there's a lot to be gained through trying to get millimeter perfect on reducing risk in as many ways as possible feels like a lot of work up front but I think it saves you a ton of time afterwards so first let's talk about how to avoid complications the first mistake which people out there must be making although I have never met one is to believe that aspirating makes you 100% safe now all the anti- aspirators in our industry always put this as one of the reasons they don't like aspirating is because of false reassurance and that it will give injectors is the idea that they don't need to worry about other safety steps now if you've met someone who's like this please let me know because I've never met anyone like this but I'm going to say it here because maybe there is one out there somewhere that if you aspirate and you get a negative it doesn't mean that you're not in a vessel it just means there's a lower probability that you're in a vessel at that moment but there's a lot of other things you should be doing as well to make yourself safe and it's these layering on of safety steps which really is the secret to Safe injecting number two the opposite of this which is not aspirating and thinking that you're

safe because you heard some guy saying that aspirating doesn't work this means you're using just one other thing for example like I'm going to continuously move my needle without anyone telling you exactly what proportion of your filler at different layers of the skin that you can inject before it's a risk without knowing the volume of the vessels without knowing how many different entry points or how much movement you need to or how widely you have to dissipate your filler in order for it to be safe but just thinking while I'm doing the moving that means I'm safe this is another short fire away to find yourself in a niche situation where you just cause a vasc clusion because you haven't got all the parameters sorted out and you're relying on one safety measure a while ago I shot this funny video which uses the analogy of a seat Bel to explain one of the logical flaws that anti- aspirators use when talking about aspirating dude what the hell I realized realize what false reassurance what do you mean false reassurance you see it hit me aspirating is dangerous because it's not 100% reliable and then I thought same with seat belts what do you

have any idea how many people get killed wearing seat belts every year and you're driving around like Iron Man thinking that you're Invincible just because you put your seat belt on that's not how it works what are you talking about it's basically exactly the same we know that if you aspirate and you get a negative that instantly makes you gung-ho about all the other safety things you can do and you just bow us away happily it's just the same with a seat Bel that's why you've been driving around like a maniac and that's not me talking that's science that's just a story you made up about me there's more to safety than just one step you've been pinning your life on that seat belt all this time driving around thinking you're Mr Invincible all the while knowing that it's not 100% reliable dude it doesn't have to be 100% reliable to still be a valid step yes it does things are either 100% reliable or useless that's science stop saying that's science your own mother wasn't 100% reliable and you didn't throw her out it's your assertion I think you should leave our mother out of this that's the evidence no it's not nobody asked me if I was 100% assured by aspirating but dude what about the science we know it's not 100% reliable device no it's not nobody asked me if I was 100% reassured by wearing a seat belt I know it's just one step of many this all sounds really nuanced it either works every time or it's got no use to

me which is why I'm driving so here's how I think you could lower your chances of having a really big accident we're going to lower the probability of us being in any particular place at any particular time by moving around so the trick is you got to keep moving it lowers your chance of a direct hit check it out why don't you just stay neatly where there's a lower chance of hitting things the concept is Flawless more frequent smaller accidents but fewer really big ones are you really saying this is safer than just my imperfect seat belt only risking a glancing blow a mere flesh wound dude this is not science I'm putting my seat belt on did not expect this oh my heart the reason it's relevant is because wearing a seat belt does not guarantee that you will not be killed in a car accident it merely reduces the risk there are also instances like for example if you fly into a Lake that wearing a seat belt is a disadvantage but we all accept on balance that wearing a seat belt is better than not

wearing a seat belt and that is the same reason why we should still aspirate despite it not being 100% effective at detecting any intravascular injection points the next mistake I see injectors make even world famous injectors is relying purely on anatomy and this looks like that guy on stage who puts a full Mill into someone's forehead in one bolus because he thinks the arteries never run on the periosteum in the forehead it's true that it's very unlikely you'll find a large vessel in the middle of the forehead and you probably can get away with a bolus and massage in this way but every now and then you're going to meet a patient who doesn't fit the textbook and in fact this happened to me when I first posted some an anatomy learning online and I got a message from a surgeon in America called Patricia ooli who sent me a picture of a forehead she dissected which showed basically the full periosteum covered in vessels it really is quite a shocking image if you've been relying on there never being a vessel there and what this means is relying on the statistical probability of where

vessels should be is never enough for a lifetime's worth of practice you always have to inject in a way that makes it safer even if there is some abant anatomy and that is a really common mistake I see the same thing being said about cheeks you'll never find a blood vessel on the periosteum of the cheek so you don't need to aspirate I've got a member of my Mastery Program who recently sent a full syringer blood through in a picture showing that she had aspirated on the cheek and got a huge positive aspiration which proves that there was a vessel where there shouldn't have been one this is common also in chins where we are taught that injecting on the midline is fine because you'll never find a vessel there only recently Julie Bas Kaplan shared a large vascular clusion that she'd had in clinic not her injecting but that involved a vascular clusion that happened on the midline of the chin exactly where the vessel shouldn't be according to the textbooks it's similar in the temple where the gunshot technique is now known to have a vessel which sometimes runs directly underneath that point despite it being usually a

very safe place to inject so we cannot rely just on the anatomy the next safety mistake is to rely purely on ultrasound now this is very tempting because it does feel like I can just have a look see if there's nothing there and then inject without having to worry about it but the truth is there are many cases now of vasc clusions happening straight after an ultrasound in fact Julie Bas kaplan's case that she shared with the tongue BAS in usion that patient had been scanned prior to the injection but small changes in position of the probe or user error can make it possible to do a scan and not see a vessel and then to be I mean how unlucky can you get but also to get a patient with aberant Anatomy the combination of all these factors lined up to cause a rather large vascular occlusion but thankfully Julie had all the other safety protocols in place so that patient didn't get any necrotic wound which is a fantastic outcome but it just goes to show you cannot rely on any one of these safety measures next is diagnosing a vascular clusion because if you diagnose a vascular clusion immediately you are in a very good position to prevent necrosis

especially of course if you've used a reversible product a hyaluronic acid as opposed to one of these non-reversible products now this is absolutely key to get the diagnosis right to start with when I had my first vasc inclusion many years ago I remember noting the capillary refill initially looked normal as I gently pushed on the lip as I increased the pressure because I could sense something wasn't right I kept pushing harder and for longer it became really obvious that there was a compromise to that vessel not long ago Dr subbio posted a fantastic clip where he demonstrated a normal capillary refill then as his camera panned out it was revealed that this normal capillary refill was actually on a chunk of skin that had already been removed from the patient demonstrating unambiguously that it's not cardiac output that is refilling the skin in this situation and that the capillary refill that you're seeing in some cases if you're only pressing gently is probably just a little bit of blood in the Venus system that swashes in and out superficially and looks like blood flow so you must do a decent capillary refill my capil

refill tests these days involve often the full palm of my hand pushing on the patient for 5 to 10 seconds and then releasing because I find it so useful seeing which direction the blood flow comes from and also always including a control so if I'm going to compress one side of the lip I'll also compress the other side of the lip and if I'm worried about a cheek I'll do both cheeks so it's a good long f press under good bright light and then releasing and seeing where the blood flow is last to return to extremely helpful test I think it's underrated despite the fact that it's used by nearly everyone but you must do it properly so if you're interested in learning a lot more about the anatomy and how to become a safe and effective injector make sure you sign up to the waiting list for my new membership the link is in the [Music] description

and that the capillary refill that you're seeing in some cases if you're only pressing gently is probably just a little bit of blood in the Venus system that swashes in and out superficially and looks like blood flow so you must do a decent capillary refill my capil refill tests these days involve often the full palm of my hand pushing on the patient for 5 to 10 seconds and then releasing because I find it so useful seeing which direction the blood flow comes from and also always including a control so if I'm going to compress one side of the lip I'll also compress the other side of the lip and if I'm worried about a cheek I'll do both cheeks so it's a good long f press under good bright light and then releasing and seeing where the blood flow is last to return to extremely helpful test I
